common marmoset (Callithrix jacchus)

CC BY-SA 2.0 Luciano Marra (flickr)

Taksonomia

Rodzina: Callitrichidae

Rząd: Primates

Zasoby

Typ Link
PacBio CLR
10x
Bionano
Arima
Illumina

Opis

The common marmoset (Callithrix jacchus) is a small New World monkey native to northeastern Brazil. It is known for its distinctive ear tufts, social behavior, and adaptability to a variety of environments.

Key Characteristics

  • Appearance:
    • Small body size with a long, banded tail.
    • Distinctive white, tufted ears.
    • Grayish-brown fur with lighter underparts.
    • Clawed fingers (except the big toe), adapted for climbing.
  • Reproduction:
    • Typically gives birth to twins.
    • Cooperative breeding: group members help care for the young.
    • Females can reproduce multiple times per year.
  • Habitat:
    • Tropical and subtropical forests, including dry forests and secondary growth.
    • Often found in areas impacted by human activity.
    • Lives in trees and rarely comes to the ground.
  • Diet:
    • Omnivorous: feeds on tree sap, insects, fruits, and small vertebrates.
    • Uses specialized teeth to gouge tree bark and stimulate sap flow.
  • Behavior:
    • Highly social and lives in family groups.
    • Communicates using vocalizations, facial expressions, and scent marking.
    • Diurnal (active during the day).

Unique Adaptations

  • Gouging Dentition: Specialized lower incisors allow the marmoset to gnaw into tree bark and access sap and gum.
  • Clawed Digits: Unlike most primates, they have claw-like nails (tegulae) that help them cling to tree trunks.
  • Cooperative Care: Group members, including males, actively participate in carrying and feeding offspring.
  • High Reproductive Rate: Frequent births and twin offspring increase survival chances.
  • Territorial Behavior: Uses scent glands to mark territory and communicate with group members.

Conservation Status and Threats

  • Least Concern: Classified as “Least Concern” by the IUCN due to its wide distribution and adaptability.
  • Threats:
    • Habitat destruction from deforestation and urban expansion.
    • Capture for the pet trade.
    • Competition and hybridization with introduced marmoset species.
    • Use in biomedical research.

Interesting Facts

  • The common marmoset is one of the smallest primates in the world.
  • It can rotate its head up to 180 degrees.
  • Twins are the most common form of offspring.
  • It relies heavily on tree sap, which is unusual among primates.
  • Marmosets communicate with high-pitched calls that can be heard over long distances.

Conservation Efforts

  • Protection of natural habitats and forest reserves.
  • Regulation of wildlife trade.
  • Monitoring of wild populations.
  • Research on behavior, genetics, and ecology.
  • Public education and awareness programs.

2026 Genome Ark. Autor: Gabriela Pawlak
